goal: capability registry core (Session 3.1): resolveCapability(name, context) resolves the best resolver from a static JSON registry, validates on load, and distinguishes required vs optional providers

openQuestions.question: Should Stage 3 Sessions 3.2-3.4 gaps found in review (disable-provider enabled:false not implemented; when-clause and/or/not composability not wired into registry resolution; resolved:null vs omitted-key inconsistency between capability-resolve and state-next CLIs; has-typescript condition ignores .ts-file-present branch) become separate GitHub issues, or should the 'capability resolution' goal be narrowed to Session 3.1 scope only (already fully verified)?
openQuestions.answer: Superseded — reopened as a blocking question (was incorrectly opened non-blocking; a PR reviewer flagged that this let 'octowiz state next' route to handoff-or-ship despite unresolved scope)

openQuestions.question: Should Stage 3 Sessions 3.2-3.4 gaps found in review (disable-provider enabled:false not implemented; when-clause and/or/not composability not wired into registry resolution; resolved:null vs omitted-key inconsistency between capability-resolve and state-next CLIs; has-typescript condition ignores .ts-file-present branch) become separate GitHub issues, or should the 'capability resolution' goal be narrowed to Session 3.1 scope only (already fully verified)?
openQuestions.answer: Narrow the goal to Session 3.1 scope only (capability registry core: loader, schema validation, resolver) — already fully verified. Sessions 3.2-3.4 gaps (provider disable enabled:false, when-clause composability wiring, resolved:null vs omitted-key consistency, has-typescript .ts scan) are out of scope for this goal; file as separate follow-up work if/when picked up.

acceptanceCriteria.statement: resolveCapability(name, context) returns best resolver or null
evidenceRefs: src/capabilities/registry.js:resolveCapability; verified: resolveCapability(registry,'implementation',{}) returns resolver, unknown name returns null

acceptanceCriteria.statement: unknown capabilities return null, not an error
registry validates on load; malformed registries throw at startup
evidenceRefs: src/capabilities/schema.js:validateRegistry; verified: malformed registry ({providers:'not-an-object'}) throws at load

acceptanceCriteria.statement: provider availability checked: required vs optional
all capability names from src/state/next.js have registry entries
evidenceRefs: verified: all 12 capability names referenced in src/state/next.js resolve against skills/registry.json, 0 missing

leanGate.decision: static JSON registry loaded with Node fs, hand-rolled validation — no schema engine needed
rejectedAlternatives: JSON Schema library, database, dynamic plugin system

items.ref: jest: 43 suites, 718 tests, 0 failures (tests/capabilities/*.test.js + full suite)
eslint . : 0 errors, 52 pre-existing jsdoc warnings unrelated to capability-registry work

Two-axis review (Standards+Spec) of 372b484^..372b484 vs docs/implementation-plan-stages-3-4-5.md. All 5 tracked Session-3.1 ACs confirmed correctly implemented. Review also surfaced non-blocking follow-ups outside tracked scope: Session 3.4 'disable a provider (enabled:false)' AC not implemented; Session 3.2 when-clause composability (and/or/not) not wired into registry resolution, JS-only; Session 3.3 resolved:null vs omitted-key inconsistency between capability-resolve CLI and state-next CLI; has-typescript condition ignores .ts-file-present branch of its own spec line; latent priority ?? Infinity vs ?? 1 fallback inconsistency (dormant, no current registry entry omits priority)
